result.markdown:
--- title: "Building a Bulk Email Dispatcher in Shiny" description: "A reproducible walkthrough for sending hundreds of personalised HTML emails from a Shiny app — with rate limiting, test mode, a live progress bar, and Outlook COM automation." author: "Andrew Fuller" date: "2026-05-20" categories: [R, Shiny, Email, Tutorial] image: "thumbnail.png" toc: true toc-depth: 3 --- Suppose you need to send a few hundred personalised emails every month — each one containing a colour-coded summary table tailored to the recipient. Maybe it's monthly performance reports going to regional office managers, or data submission reminders going to partner organisations. You *could* sit in Outlook and copy-paste. Or you could build a Shiny app that handles the whole workflow: previewing the campaign, test-sending to yourself, and dispatching the real thing with a live progress bar and built-in rate limiting. This post is a fully reproducible walkthrough of how to build one. Every code block below uses synthetic data you can generate yourself — no external database or credentials required to follow along. *** ## Setup Install the packages we'll use: ```r install.packages(c( "shiny", "dplyr", "gt", "rmarkdown", "blastula", "waiter", "shinyalert", "shinyjs" )) # RDCOMClient is needed for Outlook COM automation (Windows only). # Install from GitHub if not already available: # install.packages("RDCOMClient", repos = "https://nexus.cs.ucdavis.edu/repository/r-proxy/") ``` ::: {.callout-note} `RDCOMClient` only works on Windows with Outlook installed. If you're on macOS/Linux or want a portable solution, skip to [What I'd Do Differently] at the end for SMTP alternatives. ::: *** ## Step 1: Create Synthetic Data First, let's generate some fake data to work with — a set of regional offices, each with monthly episode counts and contact details. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="false"} library(dplyr) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Attaching package: 'dplyr' ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': filter, lag ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` The following objects are masked from 'package:base': intersect, setdiff, setequal, union ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="false"} set.seed(42) # 20 regional offices across two programme types offices <- tibble( office_id = 1:20, office_name = paste("Region", LETTERS[1:20], "Office"), programme = rep(c("Inpatient", "Outpatient"), each = 10), is_active = sample(c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE), 20, replace = TRUE) ) # Monthly submission counts for the last 12 months months_cols <- format(seq.Date(as.Date("2025-06-01"), by = "month", length.out = 12), "%b-%y") submissions <- offices |> rowwise() |> mutate(data = list(setNames( as.list(sample(c(0, 0, rpois(10, lambda = 15)), 12, replace = TRUE)), months_cols ))) |> ungroup() |> tidyr::unnest_wider(data) # Contact list — some offices have multiple contacts contacts <- bind_rows( tibble( office_id = rep(1:20, each = 2), name = paste("Contact", 1:40), email = paste0("user", 1:40, "@example.com") ) ) |> # drop a few to simulate missing contacts slice_sample(prop = 0.85) ``` ::: Here's a peek at our data: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="false"} submissions |> select(office_id, office_name, programme, all_of(months_cols[1:4])) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 20 × 7 office_id office_name programme `Jun-25` `Jul-25` `Aug-25` `Sep-25` 1 1 Region A Office Inpatient 20 18 18 23 2 2 Region B Office Inpatient 19 11 11 12 3 3 Region C Office Inpatient 17 16 11 17 4 4 Region D Office Inpatient 21 20 0 10 5 5 Region E Office Inpatient 19 0 19 0 6 6 Region F Office Inpatient 12 0 13 0 7 7 Region G Office Inpatient 21 0 14 10 8 8 Region H Office Inpatient 13 0 14 13 9 9 Region I Office Inpatient 14 15 20 12 10 10 Region J Office Inpatient 0 16 22 22 11 11 Region K Office Outpatient 14 19 10 16 12 12 Region L Office Outpatient 14 10 14 9 13 13 Region M Office Outpatient 13 7 7 13 14 14 Region N Office Outpatient 16 18 0 24 15 15 Region O Office Outpatient 13 13 16 18 16 16 Region P Office Outpatient 13 0 17 17 17 17 Region Q Office Outpatient 17 17 14 12 18 18 Region R Office Outpatient 14 14 15 20 19 19 Region S Office Outpatient 14 16 17 20 20 20 Region T Office Outpatient 9 12 0 16 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="false"} contacts |> head(8) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 8 × 3 office_id name email 1 10 Contact 20 user20@example.com 2 20 Contact 39 user39@example.com 3 5 Contact 9 user9@example.com 4 10 Contact 19 user19@example.com 5 11 Contact 21 user21@example.com 6 9 Contact 17 user17@example.com 7 17 Contact 34 user34@example.com 8 4 Contact 7 user7@example.com ``` ::: ::: *** ## Step 2: Building the Email Plan Before sending anything, we build a **plan** — one row per office, with the recipient addresses and the data slice that will go into that office's email. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="false"} prepare_email_plan <- function( submissions, contacts, filter_active = TRUE, filter_programme = NULL ) { data <- submissions if (filter_active) data <- data |> filter(is_active) if (!is.null(filter_programme)) data <- data |> filter(programme == filter_programme) # One row per office, with a pre-sliced data frame and contact list plan <- data |> group_by(office_id, office_name, programme) |> summarise(across(everything(), first), .groups = "drop") |> rowwise() |> mutate( office_contacts = list(contacts |> filter(office_id == .env$office_id)), to_emails = paste( unique(office_contacts$email[!is.na(office_contacts$email)]), collapse = ";" ), contact_count = n_distinct(office_contacts$email[!is.na(office_contacts$email)]) ) |> ungroup() list( plan = plan, stats = list( total_offices = nrow(plan), total_contacts = sum(plan$contact_count), by_programme = plan |> group_by(programme) |> summarise(offices = n(), contacts = sum(contact_count), .groups = "drop") ) ) } result <- prepare_email_plan(submissions, contacts) result$stats ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` $total_offices [1] 15 $total_contacts [1] 26 $by_programme # A tibble: 2 × 3 programme offices contacts 1 Inpatient 8 15 2 Outpatient 7 11 ``` ::: ::: Key design choices: 1. **Pre-sliced data** — each row carries only that office's data, so the email template never touches the full dataset 2. **Contact counts drive rate limiting** — one "office email" going to 3 people counts as 3 sends against the rate limit (more on this below) 3. **Filtering is parameterised** — the Shiny UI can toggle active-only, by programme, etc. *** ## Step 3: The Email Template Each email is rendered from a parameterised R Markdown document that produces an HTML email with a colour-coded summary table. Create a file called `email_template.Rmd`: **Front matter** (`email_template.Rmd`): ```yaml # email_template.Rmd output: blastula::blastula_email params: office_name: "Example Office" programme: "Inpatient" report_data: NULL deadline: "2026-08-14" ``` **Template body** (the single R chunk inside the `.Rmd`): ```r library(gt) library(dplyr) report_data <- params$report_data # Identify the month columns (everything that looks like "Mon-YY") month_cols <- grep("^[A-Z][a-z]{2}-\\d{2}$", names(report_data), value = TRUE) tbl <- report_data |> select(office_name, programme, all_of(month_cols)) |> gt() |> tab_header( title = paste(params$programme, "Submission Summary"), subtitle = paste("Deadline:", params$deadline) ) |> cols_label(office_name = "Office", programme = "Programme") # Colour-code: red = 0, amber ≤ 5, green ≥ 15 for (col in month_cols) { tbl <- tbl |> tab_style( style = list(cell_text(weight = "bold"), cell_fill(color = "#ffc7ce")), locations = cells_body(columns = !!col, rows = .data[[col]] == 0) ) |> tab_style( style = list(cell_text(weight = "bold"), cell_fill(color = "#ffeb9c")), locations = cells_body(columns = !!col, rows = .data[[col]] > 0 & .data[[col]] <= 5) ) |> tab_style( style = list(cell_text(weight = "bold"), cell_fill(color = "#c6efce")), locations = cells_body(columns = !!col, rows = .data[[col]] >= 15) ) } tbl ``` The colour thresholds are simple here (0 / ≤5 / ≥15) but you can adjust them to whatever makes sense for your domain. The important thing is that the template is *parameterised* — the calling code passes in the data slice, and the template just renders it. ::: {.callout-tip} `blastula::blastula_email` produces self-contained HTML that renders well in most email clients. The `{gt}` table's CSS is inlined automatically. ::: *** ## Step 4: Sending with Rate Limiting Microsoft 365 enforces a sending rate of roughly 30 messages per minute for most tenants. To stay safely within that, we target **250 emails per hour** (≈ 1 every 14.4 seconds). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="false"} send_all_emails <- function( plan, test_mode = FALSE, test_email = "your-test-inbox@example.com", target_rate_per_hour = 250, session = NULL ) { sleep_per_email <- (60 * 60) / target_rate_per_hour # 14.4 seconds total <- nrow(plan) sent <- 0 failed <- 0 for (i in seq_len(total)) { row <- plan[i, ] tryCatch({ # 1. Render the email template for this office tmp_rmd <- tempfile(fileext = ".Rmd") file.copy("email_template.Rmd", tmp_rmd) rmarkdown::render( tmp_rmd, params = list( office_name = row$office_name, programme = row$programme, report_data = row, # the pre-sliced single-row data frame deadline = "2026-08-14" ), quiet = TRUE ) email_html <- paste(readLines(sub("\\.Rmd$", ".html", tmp_rmd)), collapse = "\n") # 2. Determine recipients to <- if (test_mode) test_email else row$to_emails subject <- paste0( if (test_mode) "[TEST] ", row$programme, " submission summary — ", row$office_name ) # 3. Send via Outlook COM automation outlook <- RDCOMClient::COMCreate("Outlook.Application") mail <- outlook$CreateItem(0) # 0 = MailItem mail[["To"]] <- to mail[["Subject"]] <- subject mail[["HTMLBody"]] <- email_html mail$Send() sent <- sent + 1 # 4. Update progress (if inside a Shiny session) if (!is.null(session)) { session$sendCustomMessage("updateProgress", list( current = i, total = total, percent = round((i / total) * 100, 1), message = paste("Sent to", row$office_name) )) } # 5. Rate-limit sleep — scaled by number of contacts Sys.sleep(sleep_per_email * max(row$contact_count, 1)) }, error = function(e) { failed <<- failed + 1 message(sprintf("[%d/%d] FAILED: %s — %s", i, total, row$office_name, e$message)) }) } list(sent = sent, failed = failed, total = total) } ``` ::: A few things to unpack: ### Rate limiting math The `Sys.sleep()` at the end of each iteration scales by `contact_count` — because an email going to 3 people counts as 3 sends against Microsoft's rate limit: ```r sleep_per_email <- (60 * 60) / 250 # = 14.4 seconds per "send" # Office with 3 contacts → sleep 14.4 × 3 = 43.2 seconds Sys.sleep(sleep_per_email * row$contact_count) ``` ### Outlook COM automation `RDCOMClient` drives Outlook as a COM object. The minimum required: ```r outlook <- RDCOMClient::COMCreate("Outlook.Application") mail <- outlook$CreateItem(0) # 0 = MailItem mail[["To"]] <- "a@example.com;b@example.com" # semicolon-separated mail[["Subject"]] <- "Your monthly report" mail[["HTMLBody"]] <- "

Hello

" mail$Send() ``` Requirements: - Outlook must be installed on the machine running the app - The R process must run as a user with an Outlook profile configured - If using `SentOnBehalfOfName`, the user needs delegate permissions for that address *** ## Step 5: Test Mode Before sending to real recipients, you want a **test mode** that redirects everything to your own inbox. The pattern is simple — thread a single `test_mode` boolean through the entire stack: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="false"} # When test_mode = TRUE: to <- if (test_mode) test_email else row$to_emails subject <- paste0(if (test_mode) "[TEST] ", subject) ``` ::: In a Shiny context, gate the send behind a confirmation dialog: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="false"} shinyalert::shinyalert( title = "Send Test Campaign?", text = htmltools::tagList( htmltools::div( class = "alert alert-info", htmltools::h4("TEST MODE — SAFE TO SEND"), htmltools::p( htmltools::strong("All emails will be sent to: "), htmltools::code("your-test-inbox@example.com") ) ), htmltools::p("\u2713 Subject lines prefixed with [TEST]"), htmltools::p("\u2713 No database updates will be made") ), html = TRUE, type = "info", showCancelButton = TRUE, callbackR = function(confirmed) { if (confirmed) { send_all_emails(plan, test_mode = TRUE, test_email = "your-test-inbox@example.com") } } ) ``` ::: When `test_mode = TRUE`: 1. **Recipients** are replaced with your sandbox address 2. **Subject lines** get a `[TEST]` prefix 3. **Database updates are skipped** — no flags are flipped, so the real campaign can still run This lets you send 5 test emails to yourself, verify them in Outlook, and then dispatch the real campaign knowing nothing has been touched. *** ## Step 6: Live Progress Feedback The trickiest UX piece: showing progress while R is blocked inside a `for` loop. The approach uses the `{waiter}` package for a full-screen overlay, with a custom HTML progress bar that gets updated via Shiny's `sendCustomMessage()`: ### R side — send updates from the loop ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="false"} # Inside the send loop (already shown in Step 4): session$sendCustomMessage("updateProgress", list( current = i, total = total, percent = round((i / total) * 100, 1), message = paste("Sent to", row$office_name) )) ``` ::: ### JavaScript side — receive and render Add this to your app's `www/` directory (or inline via `tags$script()`): ```javascript Shiny.addCustomMessageHandler("updateProgress", function(data) { var text = document.getElementById("progress-text"); var bar = document.getElementById("progress-bar"); var msg = document.getElementById("progress-message"); if (text) text.textContent = data.current + " / " + data.total + " emails sent"; if (bar) bar.style.width = data.percent + "%"; if (msg) msg.textContent = data.message; }); ``` ### Waiter overlay setup ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="false"} library(waiter) create_progress_waiter <- function() { Waiter$new( html = htmltools::tagList( htmltools::div( class = "waiter-content-center", htmltools::h4(id = "progress-text", "Preparing to send emails..."), htmltools::div( class = "progress", style = "width: 80%; margin: 20px auto;", htmltools::div( id = "progress-bar", class = "progress-bar progress-bar-striped active", role = "progressbar", style = "width: 0%; transition: width 0.4s ease;" ) ), htmltools::p(id = "progress-message", "Initialising...") ) ), color = "rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.92)", fadeout = TRUE ) } ``` ::: The result: a full-screen overlay that smoothly updates with the office name and a percentage bar as each email is dispatched. *** ## Estimated Wait Time Before confirming a production send, show the user how long they'll be waiting: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="false"} # Using our earlier plan total_contacts <- result$stats$total_contacts seconds_per_email <- (60 * 60) / 250 # 14.4 seconds wait_seconds <- total_contacts * seconds_per_email wait_minutes <- wait_seconds / 60 cat(sprintf( "Sending to %d contacts across %d offices.\nEstimated time: %s", total_contacts, result$stats$total_offices, if (wait_minutes >= 60) paste0(round(wait_minutes / 60, 1), " hours") else paste0(round(wait_minutes, 1), " minutes") )) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Sending to 26 contacts across 15 offices. Estimated time: 6.2 minutes ``` ::: ::: *** ## Putting It All Together Here's a minimal but complete Shiny app that ties these pieces into a three-step workflow: **Build Plan → Test Send → Production Send**. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Full Shiny app (click to expand)"} library(shiny) library(shinyjs) library(shinyalert) library(waiter) library(dplyr) ui <- fluidPage( useShinyjs(), useShinyalert(), useWaiter(), tags$head(tags$script(HTML(' Shiny.addCustomMessageHandler("updateProgress", function(data) { var text = document.getElementById("progress-text"); var bar = document.getElementById("progress-bar"); var msg = document.getElementById("progress-message"); if (text) text.textContent = data.current + " / " + data.total + " emails sent"; if (bar) bar.style.width = data.percent + "%"; if (msg) msg.textContent = data.message; }); '))), titlePanel("Bulk Email Dispatcher"), sidebarLayout( sidebarPanel( checkboxInput("active_only", "Active offices only", TRUE), selectInput("programme", "Programme", choices = c("All", "Inpatient", "Outpatient"), selected = "All"), textInput("test_email", "Test inbox", value = "your-test-inbox@example.com"), hr(), actionButton("btn_plan", "1. Build Plan", class = "btn-primary", width = "100%"), br(), br(), actionButton("btn_test", "2. Test Send", class = "btn-warning", width = "100%"), br(), br(), actionButton("btn_send", "3. Send Production", class = "btn-danger", width = "100%") ), mainPanel( verbatimTextOutput("plan_summary"), tableOutput("plan_table") ) ) ) server <- function(input, output, session) { rv <- reactiveValues(plan = NULL, stats = NULL) observeEvent(input$btn_plan, { prog_filter <- if (input$programme == "All") NULL else input$programme result <- prepare_email_plan(submissions, contacts, filter_active = input$active_only, filter_programme = prog_filter) rv$plan <- result$plan rv$stats <- result$stats }) output$plan_summary <- renderPrint({ req(rv$stats) cat(sprintf("Offices: %d | Contacts: %d | Est. time: %.1f min\n", rv$stats$total_offices, rv$stats$total_contacts, rv$stats$total_contacts * 14.4 / 60)) print(rv$stats$by_programme) }) output$plan_table <- renderTable({ req(rv$plan) rv$plan |> select(office_name, programme, to_emails, contact_count) }) observeEvent(input$btn_test, { req(rv$plan) shinyalert("Test Send?", paste("All emails will go to:", input$test_email), type = "info", showCancelButton = TRUE, callbackR = function(ok) { if (ok) send_all_emails(rv$plan, test_mode = TRUE, test_email = input$test_email, session = session) }) }) observeEvent(input$btn_send, { req(rv$plan) shinyalert("Send Production?", paste(rv$stats$total_contacts, "emails will be sent to REAL recipients."), type = "warning", showCancelButton = TRUE, callbackR = function(ok) { if (ok) send_all_emails(rv$plan, test_mode = FALSE, session = session) }) }) } shinyApp(ui, server) ``` ::: *** ## What I'd Do Differently 1. **Use SMTP instead of COM** — `RDCOMClient` requires Outlook on the server, which is awkward in containers or on non-Windows machines. [`Microsoft365R`](https://github.com/Azure/Microsoft365R) provides a Graph API client that works anywhere, or you can use a proper SMTP relay with `{blastula}` or `{emayili}`. 2. **Run the send loop asynchronously** — `send_all_emails()` blocks the R process. Wrapping it in `future::future()` with `promises` would keep the Shiny session responsive, though pushing progress updates from a background process takes extra plumbing (e.g., writing progress to a file and polling with `reactivePoll()`). 3. **Add resume-from-failure** — if the loop errors at email 150 of 200, you currently have to re-run the whole campaign (relying on an "exclude already sent" filter). An explicit checkpoint — writing sent IDs to a log file and skipping them on restart — would be more robust. *** ## Packages Used | Package | Role | |---|---| | `RDCOMClient` | Outlook COM automation (Windows only) | | `rmarkdown` | Render the HTML email template per office | | `blastula` | `blastula_email` output format for email-friendly HTML | | `gt` | Colour-coded summary table inside the email | | `waiter` | Full-screen loading overlay during send | | `shinyalert` | Confirmation dialogs (test vs production) | | `shinyjs` | Disable/enable buttons during the send loop | | `dplyr` | Data wrangling and plan construction | *** All the code above is self-contained — generate the synthetic data, create the template file, and you've got a working email dispatcher. Swap in your real data source, adjust the `{gt}` thresholds, and you're production-ready. If you're building something similar, feel free to reach out via [GitHub](https://github.com/andrew-fuller).
